current_focus: Treat K=128 only as the initial search anchor, not as a success criterion. The research objective is to find configurations whose FVU is dramatically better than the current K=128 baseline, with a target on the order of halving that baseline FVU before rewarding smaller K or lower cost.
performance_findings: round 3: The first-step timeout is caused by serialized DDP startup for local artifacts rather than by the K=128 TopK/Adam configuration itself; allowing both ranks to load the local model and dataset in parallel should get the proxy run to first metrics. -> keep (fvu=0.2119751125574112, observed_fvu=0.220770, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)

failure_patterns.pattern: At fixed K=128, the current default launch is likely signum-tuned; switching the mainline TopK SAE to Adam with a lower learning rate should establish a stronger quality anchor before spending rounds on lower-K or new families.

The round-1 crash was a startup-latency issue rather than a verdict on K=128 TopK quality; the most likely blocker is heavy launch overhead from compiling all transformer layers before the first step, so disabling compile and reducing preprocessing worker fanout should get a clean proxy measurement.

topk.design_hypothesis: Orthogonality regularization did not improve the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next within-family quality intervention is to train the K=128 model to remain reconstructive under slightly smaller nested supports; a lightly weighted matryoshka objective may sharpen feature ranking and improve full-K FVU.
next_steps: If TopK + matryoshka improves proxy quality, promote it and tune the nested K set or weights while keeping evaluation focused on K=128 quality.
If TopK + matryoshka is neutral or worse, stop incremental TopK regularizer search and implement a genuinely new architecture family with a sharper allocation mechanism.
If the matryoshka run helps quality but hurts throughput, keep it as a quality lead and follow with a targeted cost/perf cleanup later.

factorized_topk.design_hypothesis: The new K=128 TopK+Adam anchor at FVU 0.211975 is strong enough to stop local optimizer tinkering for now; a more expressive nonlinear encoder is the next plausible path toward a materially lower FVU, so `factorized_topk` should be tested at the same K=128 anchor settings.
next_steps: If `factorized_topk` shows materially better proxy FVU at K=128, promote it and then tune its encoder width or related family-specific capacity.
If `factorized_topk` is neutral or worse, try another expressive encoder family such as `mixture_topk` rather than returning immediately to smaller-K search.
If the new family is quality-promising but noticeably slower, treat that as a performance-fix follow-up instead of an immediate architectural rejection.

mixture_topk.design_hypothesis: `factorized_topk` missed badly at K=128, so the next plausible quality-focused family is a softly routed multi-expert encoder; `mixture_topk` may improve reconstruction by adding conditional encoder capacity without the hard routing bottlenecks of grouped variants.
next_steps: If `mixture_topk` shows a clear proxy quality gain over plain TopK at K=128, promote it and then tune family-specific capacity.
If `mixture_topk` is also clearly worse than TopK, shift the next architecture probe toward a different sparse activation family such as `jumprelu` or a new routed/multi-branch design.
If `mixture_topk` is promising but slow, follow with a targeted performance pass instead of discarding the family immediately.

jumprelu.design_hypothesis: Both encoder-capacity families tried so far are far behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next coherent probe is a different sparse activation mechanism rather than more mixing capacity; `jumprelu` may improve feature selection quality by learning per-latent activation thresholds.
next_steps: If `jumprelu` is competitive in proxy, follow with a stabilization round on its threshold and bandwidth parameters before promotion.
If `jumprelu` is also clearly worse than TopK, move to another qualitatively different family such as `gated` or a new multi-branch sparse encoder.
If `jumprelu` is quality-promising but unstable, keep the family incubating and tune its activation hyperparameters rather than discarding it immediately.

gated.design_hypothesis: The explored nonlinear, mixture, and thresholded families are all well behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next clean probe is `gated`, which changes selection dynamics by separating gate and magnitude branches and could improve active-feature choice without requiring a brand-new codepath.
next_steps: If `gated` is competitive in proxy, promote it or tune its optimization settings before broader conclusions.

If `gated` is also clearly worse than TopK, stop cycling through near-neighbor families and either try a stronger TopK-quality regularization pass or implement a genuinely new routed/multi-branch family.
If `gated` is quality-promising but slower, keep it incubating and follow with a targeted performance pass.

recent_rounds.hypothesis: At fixed K=128, the current default launch is likely signum-tuned; switching the mainline TopK SAE to Adam with a lower learning rate should establish a stronger quality anchor before spending rounds on lower-K or new families.

The round-1 crash was a startup-latency issue rather than a verdict on K=128 TopK quality; the most likely blocker is heavy launch overhead from compiling all transformer layers before the first step, so disabling compile and reducing preprocessing worker fanout should get a clean proxy measurement.

The first-step timeout is caused by serialized DDP startup for local artifacts rather than by the K=128 TopK/Adam configuration itself; allowing both ranks to load the local model and dataset in parallel should get the proxy run to first metrics.

The new K=128 TopK+Adam anchor at FVU 0.211975 is strong enough to stop local optimizer tinkering for now; a more expressive nonlinear encoder is the next plausible path toward a materially lower FVU, so `factorized_topk` should be tested at the same K=128 anchor settings.

`factorized_topk` missed badly at K=128, so the next plausible quality-focused family is a softly routed multi-expert encoder; `mixture_topk` may improve reconstruction by adding conditional encoder capacity without the hard routing bottlenecks of grouped variants.

Both encoder-capacity families tried so far are far behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next coherent probe is a different sparse activation mechanism rather than more mixing capacity; `jumprelu` may improve feature selection quality by learning per-latent activation thresholds.

The explored nonlinear, mixture, and thresholded families are all well behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next clean probe is `gated`, which changes selection dynamics by separating gate and magnitude branches and could improve active-feature choice without requiring a brand-new codepath.

The near-neighbor architecture probes are all far worse than the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next best bet is to push quality within the winning family; adding decoder orthogonality pressure may improve basis diversity and reduce FVU for TopK at fixed K=128.

Orthogonality regularization did not improve the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next within-family quality intervention is to train the K=128 model to remain reconstructive under slightly smaller nested supports; a lightly weighted matryoshka objective may sharpen feature ranking and improve full-K FVU.

recent_insights: Round 0 should establish a trustworthy K=128 quality anchor before rewarding smaller K.
Use Adam with a lower LR as the first clean mainline retune; do not mix in lower-K objectives yet.

round 1: At fixed K=128, the current default launch is likely signum-tuned; switching the mainline TopK SAE to Adam with a lower learning rate should establish a stronger quality anchor before spending rounds on lower-K or new families. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=, k=None, health=crash, termination=first_step_timeout)
Round 1 produced a first_step_timeout crash before any quality signal, so do not draw a negative conclusion about Adam or TopK from it.
Treat this as a startup/perf unblock attempt to recover a trustworthy K=128 anchor measurement.

round 2: The round-1 crash was a startup-latency issue rather than a verdict on K=128 TopK quality; the most likely blocker is heavy launch overhead from compiling all transformer layers before the first step, so disabling compile and reducing preprocessing worker fanout should get a clean proxy measurement. -> crash (fvu=None, observed_fvu=None, k=None, health=crash, termination=invalid_env_overrides)
Round 1 crash still has no usable FVU signal; treat it as startup-path failure, not as evidence against TopK or Adam.
Local DDP artifact loading was serialized in sparsify/__main__.py; this round parallelizes local-path startup across ranks to reduce first-step latency.

round 3: The first-step timeout is caused by serialized DDP startup for local artifacts rather than by the K=128 TopK/Adam configuration itself; allowing both ranks to load the local model and dataset in parallel should get the proxy run to first metrics. -> keep (fvu=0.2119751125574112, observed_fvu=0.220770, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
A trustworthy K=128 quality anchor now exists: full TopK+Adam at FVU 0.2119751125574112.
With the anchor established and still far from the aggressive target, bias subsequent rounds toward architecture exploration rather than more local TopK retuning.

round 4: The new K=128 TopK+Adam anchor at FVU 0.211975 is strong enough to stop local optimizer tinkering for now; a more expressive nonlinear encoder is the next plausible path toward a materially lower FVU, so `factorized_topk` should be tested at the same K=128 anchor settings. -> incubate (fvu=0.6381136849522591, observed_fvu=0.642827, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)

`factorized_topk` prototype quality was poor at K=128 proxy (FVU 0.6381136849522591) despite normal runtime, so simple nonlinear hidden mixing does not currently look like a path to the aggressive target.
Keep architecture exploration focused on families with qualitatively different encoder behavior rather than continuing local retuning of the weak `factorized_topk` prototype.

round 5: `factorized_topk` missed badly at K=128, so the next plausible quality-focused family is a softly routed multi-expert encoder; `mixture_topk` may improve reconstruction by adding conditional encoder capacity without the hard routing bottlenecks of grouped variants. -> incubate (fvu=0.4306887313723564, observed_fvu=0.432906, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
`mixture_topk` improved over `factorized_topk` but remained far behind the mainline K=128 TopK anchor, so extra encoder mixture capacity alone is not closing the quality gap.
Continue exploring qualitatively different architecture families at fixed K=128 until one shows a real path below the 0.211975 anchor.

round 6: Both encoder-capacity families tried so far are far behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next coherent probe is a different sparse activation mechanism rather than more mixing capacity; `jumprelu` may improve feature selection quality by learning per-latent activation thresholds. -> archive (fvu=0.4106803648173809, observed_fvu=0.412232, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
`jumprelu` at default hyperparameters matched the weak proxy band of other alternative families and did not show a plausible path below the K=128 TopK anchor.

The architecture search so far suggests that modest encoder expressivity changes are not enough; remaining probes should either be qualitatively different or push back toward stronger TopK-family quality interventions.

round 7: The explored nonlinear, mixture, and thresholded families are all well behind the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next clean probe is `gated`, which changes selection dynamics by separating gate and magnitude branches and could improve active-feature choice without requiring a brand-new codepath. -> incubate (fvu=0.5799434706568718, observed_fvu=0.581465, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
The current set of alternative families is uniformly far behind TopK at K=128 proxy, so weakly modified encoder families are not presently challenging the mainline anchor.
Shift the search back to quality-focused TopK-family interventions until there is evidence for a genuinely stronger new architecture idea.

round 8: The near-neighbor architecture probes are all far worse than the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next best bet is to push quality within the winning family; adding decoder orthogonality pressure may improve basis diversity and reduce FVU for TopK at fixed K=128. -> discard (fvu=0.4117162860929966, observed_fvu=0.413157, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
TopK + orthogonality regularization failed to improve proxy quality at K=128, so simple decoder-basis regularization is not the missing ingredient.
Continue K=128 quality work with interventions aimed at better latent ranking or allocation rather than rewarding lower-K endpoints directly.

round 9: Orthogonality regularization did not improve the K=128 TopK anchor, so the next within-family quality intervention is to train the K=128 model to remain reconstructive under slightly smaller nested supports; a lightly weighted matryoshka objective may sharpen feature ranking and improve full-K FVU. -> archive (fvu=0.4099150709807873, observed_fvu=0.411481, k=128, health=normal, termination=completed)
next_hypotheses: If TopK + matryoshka improves proxy quality, promote it and tune the nested K set or weights while keeping evaluation focused on K=128 quality.
If TopK + matryoshka is neutral or worse, stop incremental TopK regularizer search and implement a genuinely new architecture family with a sharper allocation mechanism.
If the matryoshka run helps quality but hurts throughput, keep it as a quality lead and follow with a targeted cost/perf cleanup later.
